presentation_markdown:
## What this looks like
The APK contains markers typical of a security training exercise or lab environment, such as non-routable domains and placeholder package names.

## Why I'm saying that
- Uses a reserved '.example' web domain that doesn't work on the real internet
- Package name is a known placeholder for security research
- No active connection to real-world malicious servers
- Designed to demonstrate how permissions are abused in a controlled environment

## What to do now
1. Delete the APK file from your downloads folder
2. Uninstall the application if it was installed
3. Stick to official app stores for all banking needs
4. Keep your phone's security software updated

## Reporting
The application uses a non-routable .example domain and appears to be a harmless simulation for educational or testing purposes rather than a live scam.
signals_found: SEARCH: com.fake.bank.secure — Identified as a common placeholder in security training and malware analysis labs.
URL_CONTEXT: http://bad-c2.example/collectPK — Contains ZIP file central directory headers (PK\x01\x02), indicating file upload/exfiltration.
Domain uses reserved '.example' TLD, preventing public resolution.
Impersonation of a 'secure bank update' in the metadata.

osint.threat_summary: The indicators point to a simulated or training-based Android malware campaign. The use of the '.example' TLD and the package name 'com.fake.bank.secure' strongly suggests a lab environment, CTF, or security training exercise rather than an active wild threat. However, the structure of the indicators (C2 collection paths, binary headers in URLs, and bank-impersonating package names) mimics real-world banking trojan behavior, specifically data exfiltration and credential harvesting.

bad-c2.example.notes: The domain '.example' is a reserved top-level domain for documentation and examples. It is not a live routable domain on the public internet.

raw.victim_advice: 1. Immediately disconnect your device from the internet (Wi-Fi and mobile data). 2. Do not log into any banking or financial apps. 3. Go to Settings > Apps and look for 'com.fake.bank.secure' or any app related to 'Bank Secure Update' and uninstall it. 4. If the app has 'Device Administrator' or 'Accessibility' rights, revoke them first before uninstalling. 5. Contact your bank immediately to freeze your accounts and reset your login credentials from a clean device. 6. Consider a factory reset of your mobile device to ensure all malicious components are removed.

However, the structure of the indicators (C2 collection paths, binary headers in URLs, and bank-impersonating package names) mimics real-world banking trojan behavior, specifically data exfiltration and credential harvesting.

raw.fir_template:
Subject: Reporting Malicious Banking Malware (APK) and Attempted Financial Fraud. 

Details: I received a link (http://bad-c2.example/collect) prompting me to download an application named 'com.fake.bank.secure'. Upon investigation, this application appears to be a fraudulent APK designed to steal banking credentials and intercept SMS/OTP data. The incident originated from a URL pointing to 'bad-c2.example'. No financial loss has been confirmed yet, but the risk level is critical as the app is designed for unauthorized account access. I am reporting this to prevent further exploitation and to request the blocking of the associated malicious domain. My device has been isolated and I am in the process of securing my bank accounts linked to the phone number. 

Evidence: URL: http://bad-c2.example/collect, Package Name: com.fake.bank.secure. Case ID: SATARK-20260408-891396C0.

calendar_event.title: URGENT: Cyber Fraud Response - Case SATARK-20260408-891396C0
calendar_event.description: Golden Hour response for APK Malware. Action: Freeze bank accounts and file complaint at https://cybercrime.gov.in. Reference Case: SATARK-20260408-891396C0

raw.plain_english_summary: This application is a highly dangerous Banking Trojan disguised as a secure bank update. It requests the 'Accessibility Service' permission, which is a common tactic used by malware to intercept screen content and automate user actions without consent. The app is specifically designed to steal One-Time Passwords (OTPs) via SMS reading, log keystrokes, and exfiltrate sensitive credentials to a hardcoded command-and-control (C2) server. The package name 'com.fake.bank.secure' explicitly confirms its intent to impersonate a legitimate financial institution.
